title: Fauxdori wraparound notebook cover
subtitle: Making a Traveller's Notebook style cover from cloth or card with elastic insert holders
excerpt: A fauxdori, a home-made Traveller's Notebook cover, is a single piece of cloth, book board, or leather-grained card wrapped around one or more pocket inserts and held closed with a length of elastic. The inserts are separate and swappable. The construction is straightforward: cut the cover, punch the holes, thread the elastic spine, add closure. The whole build takes an afternoon.
sourceNotes: The Traveller's Notebook format was commercially introduced in the early 2000s. The home-made equivalent (fauxdori) follows the same structural logic as traditional limp vellum and flexible binding covers documented in bookbinding manuals from the nineteenth century onwards. The construction method draws on general bookbinding and leatherwork practice documented in pre-1928 craft manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: A home-made Traveller's Notebook style cover. The name combines 'faux' (false) and the common shortening of 'Midori' (the commercial Traveller's Notebook brand). A flexible wraparound cover, usually of cloth or leather-look card, holding swappable notebook inserts by elastic spine cords.
A small, thin pamphlet-bound or wire-bound notebook intended to be slipped into a Traveller's Notebook cover and replaced when full. Usually A6 or passport-format (88 × 125 mm).

The elastic cord or cords that run through the spine of a fauxdori cover to hold insert notebooks. The inserts are threaded onto the elastic and can be added or removed without the cover being disassembled.

projectSchedule.label: Day 1, cut the cover and line it
Day 2, add elastic spine and closure once liner is dry

content.text: is a simple, flexible soft-cover case: a rectangle of material folded at the spine, with
cords strung through holes at the spine fold. The
thread onto these cords. Everything can be made to any size; the pattern below produces a cover for A6 inserts (148 × 105 mm).

items.name: Book cloth, or leather-grained card (approximately 340 × 160 mm for A6 cover with flaps)
items.substitutions: Thick felt; cork sheet; any flexible material at least 1 mm thick

items.name: Round elastic cord, 3 mm, in a coordinating colour
items.qty: approximately 60 cm per insert cord; 80 cm for closure band
items.substitutions: Flat elastic 5 mm wide; bungee cord for a chunkier look

attrs.title: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Use a sharp blade and metal ruler. Work on a cutting mat; book cloth cuts cleanly with one firm pass.

content.text: Cut the cover piece to 340 × 160 mm. Mark the centre spine line at 170 mm from each short end and score lightly with the bone folder and ruler.

For a lined cover: cut a lining piece from thin cartridge paper to 335 × 155 mm. Spread a thin coat of PVA over the inside of the cover, place the lining centrally, and burnish flat with the bone folder. Place under a press board and weight for at least one hour.
Pierce holes along the spine line at the following positions: 20 mm from top, 80 mm from top, 80 mm from bottom, 20 mm from bottom. Four holes total.
Thread a 60 cm length of elastic cord through the top two holes from the inside outward, leaving equal lengths on each side. Tie a knot on each end inside the cover. This cord holds one insert.
Repeat for the bottom two holes with a second cord to hold a second insert, or knot into a single ring if one insert is sufficient.

For a closure band: pierce two holes on the outside face of the back cover flap, 80 mm from the spine. Thread a 80 cm length of elastic through these holes and knot on the inside. Wrap the free end around the closed cover and secure under the knot at the front.

items.symptom: The cover is too stiff to fold flat
items.cause: Cover material is too heavy; no spine score was made.
items.fix: Score the spine line more firmly with the bone folder. Use thinner cover material, anything above 1.5 mm board will not fold without creasing badly.

items.symptom: The elastic snaps after a few weeks of use
items.cause: Cord is too fine or the knots were not secure.
items.fix: Use 3 mm round elastic (not thin round elastic). Tie two knots at each end rather than one.
